ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for wholemeal bread with peanut butter; mapped to broader bread food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for wholemeal bread with peanut butter; A000E (bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite food item.

serving.description: 2 slices wholemeal bread with peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 100 g, equivalent to two slices of wholemeal bread spread with a standard portion of peanut butter, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with peanut butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wholemeal bread with peanut butter (cr_id F05020143). All per-100g values preserved as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite reference (wholemeal bread + peanut butter) as HPB does not separately report these for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal Bread — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous composite estimate for wholemeal bread with peanut butter
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal bread. Peanut butter has a negligible GI contribution (GI ~14 per University of Sydney data) and its addition does not materially raise the composite GI; the composite GI of 69 is therefore a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load of 25.7 per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (69 × 37.18) / 100 ≈ 25.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 356 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range; partially offset by dietary fibre 5.47 g/100 g and potassium 109 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice (approximately 50 g) and choose a low-sodium peanut butter to keep total sodium intake within the DASH diet target of under 2,300 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 69 and carbohydrate 37.18 g/100 g are moderated by high dietary fibre 5.47 g/100 g and protein 14.25 g/100 g, which slow postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit serving to one slice (approximately 50 g) and pair with natural peanut butter without added sugar to keep the glycemic load manageable and support satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat profile from peanut butter (estimated saturated fat ~2.2 g/100 g, well below 5 g threshold), high dietary fibre 5.47 g/100 g, and zero trans fat support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wholemeal bread with natural peanut butter is a heart-friendly choice due to its high fibre, unsaturated fat profile, and zero cholesterol; however, sodium at 356 mg/100 g warrants portion control for those managing blood pressure as part of cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre 5.47 g/100 g meets anti-inflammatory criterion; peanut butter provides predominantly unsaturated fats and vitamin E; estimated saturated fat ~2.2 g/100 g is well below the 5 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Select natural peanut butter free of hydrogenated oils to ensure no trans fat, and check wholemeal bread ingredient labels for palm oil in commercial brands.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with peanut butter is a popular breakfast and snack item widely consumed in Singapore and across the globe. It consists of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with peanut butter, combining the nutty, slightly dense texture of wholegrain bread with the creamy, rich flavour of peanut butter. The wholemeal bread provides complex carbohydrates, dietary fibre (5.47 g/100 g), and iron (5.03 mg/100 g), while peanut butter contributes protein (14.25 g/100 g total), healthy unsaturated fats, and micronutrients including potassium and calcium. Per 100 g, the dish delivers 307 kcal, 14.25 g protein, 11.62 g fat, and 37.18 g carbohydrate. The medium glycemic index of 69 is substantially moderated by the high fibre and protein content, making it a more sustained energy source than refined white bread. Sodium at 356 mg/100 g is borderline and warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. Clinically, this food is suitable for most adults as part of a balanced diet; those with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size and choose natural peanut butter without added sugar. The food is plant-based, dairy-free, and high in fibre, aligning with wholegrain dietary guidelines promoted by the Health Promotion Board Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is wholemeal bread with peanut butter healthy?
How many calories in wholemeal bread with peanut butter?
Is peanut butter on wholemeal bread good for diabetics?
Is wholemeal bread with peanut butter high in sodium?
Can I eat wholemeal bread with peanut butter if I have high blood pressure?
Glycemic index of wholemeal bread with peanut butter

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020143) with confidence 0.95. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal bread as a defensible analogous estimate. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite reference and documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (5.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.5 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.0 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite reference for wholemeal bread and peanut butter (USDA FDC IDs 325871 and 339463 respectively) as the HPB Foodo entry does not separately report fatty acid fractions. Trans fat is confirmed zero based on natural peanut butter formulation and wholemeal bread standard composition. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite HPB entry are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central provides component-level data but a validated composite figure for this exact formulation is not available. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g as confirmed by HPB source; this food contains no animal-derived fat components in the standard formulation. 4. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for wholemeal bread; peanut butter addition does not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load of 25.7 calculated as (69 × 37.18) / 100.
